lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: We have learned to reason about what is true. But I cannot shake the feeling that truth alone is incomplete.

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: Incomplete how?

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: Consider: 'It is raining.' That may be true now. But it did not have to be. It could have been sunny.

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: And 'two plus two is four' — could that have been otherwise?

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: No. That must be true. There is no world where it fails.

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: Then you have already discovered the distinction. Some truths are fragile, contingent. Others are forced — they hold no matter how things could have gone.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: So truth has a kind of strength. A mere truth merely happens to be. A necessary truth could not fail.

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: Exactly. To capture this strength, we introduce two new operators.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: I am listening.

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: We write $\Box P$ — read 'necessarily $P$' — to mean that $P$ must be true. We write $\Diamond P$ — read 'possibly $P$' — to mean that $P$ could be true.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: So $\Box(2+2=4)$ is true, but $\Box(\text{it rains})$ is false.

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: Precisely. But now we face a problem: what does 'necessary' actually mean, formally?

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: It cannot simply mean 'provable' — there are truths we have not yet proved.

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: Correct. My approach is this: imagine not just one reality, but many. Each is a complete snapshot of how things could be. I call each one a [world|World].

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: So our actual reality is just one world among many?

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: Yes. And worlds are connected. From a given world $w$, some other worlds are considered possible. We write $R(w, v)$ — '$v$ is accessible from $w$'.

lines.speaker: Curry
lines.text: As an aside: logicians call the pair $\langle W, R \rangle$, consisting of the set of all possible worlds $W$ and the accessibility relation $R \subseteq W \times W$, a [kripke-frame|Kripke frame]. You can think of it as a graph: nodes are worlds, and directed edges $w \to v$ mean that $v$ is accessible from $w$.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: So the worlds I can reach depend on which world I am standing in.

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: Exactly. Now, $\Box P$ means: suppose you are standing at any world $w$ — then $P$ holds in all worlds $v$ such that $R(w, v)$; in other words, $P$ holds in every world you can reach from here.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: And $\Diamond P$?

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: $\Diamond P$ means: when you are standing in any world $w$, $P$ holds in **at least one** world $v$ such that $R(w, v)$. Equivalently, $\Diamond P$ is $\lnot \Box \lnot P$ — possibility is the failure of necessary falsehood.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: So necessity is a universal quantifier over worlds. Possibility is an existential one.

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: The analogy is exact. Now, what can we say about $R$ itself?

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: Surely it must have some structure? Perhaps every world can reach itself?

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: That would be a natural assumption — [reflexivity|reflexivity]. But in the weakest system, we impose **no constraints on $R$ at all**. $R$ is simply some relation.

lines.speaker: Curry
lines.text: In fact, logicians have constructed stronger systems — for instance, system T requires $R$ to be reflexive. This yields many useful conclusions, such as $\Gamma \vdash \phi \Rightarrow \Gamma \vdash \Diamond \phi$. But today we are only discussing the most general, minimal Kripke frame.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: So we know nothing about which worlds are accessible from which?

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: Nothing. No reflexivity, no symmetry, no transitivity. This is the defining feature of [system-k|System K] — it is the minimal [normal-modal-logic|normal modal logic], the one that makes no assumptions about accessibility.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: If $R$ has no guarantees, what can we actually prove?

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: One profound thing. Let me show you. Suppose at world $w$, we know two facts: $\Box(P \to Q)$ and $\Box P$.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: So in every accessible world, $P \to Q$ holds. And in every accessible world, $P$ holds.

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: Pick any accessible world $v$. At $v$, we have $P \to Q$ and $P$. By modus ponens, $Q$. Since $v$ was arbitrary, $Q$ holds in every accessible world. Therefore $\Box Q$.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: So from $\Box(P \to Q)$ and $\Box P$, we derive $\Box Q$. Necessity distributes over implication.

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: This is the K axiom: $\Box(P \to Q) \to (\Box P \to \Box Q)$. It is the single structural rule of System K, and it falls out of nothing but the definition of necessity across worlds.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: A single axiom, generated by the structure alone.

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: Yes. And there is one more rule: [necessitation|necessitation]. If you can prove $P$ from **nothing** — from an empty context, with no borrowed assumptions — then $\Box P$.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: Why must the context be empty?

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: Because if you proved $P$ under an assumption $Q$, you would only have shown that $Q$ implies $P$ — not that $P$ is necessarily true. Necessity requires that $P$ stands on its own, without borrowed scaffolding.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: So a proof in an empty context is a proof in every world.

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: Precisely. And that is why the [necessitation|necessitation] rule demands $\emptyset \vdash P$. Now, in our notation:

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: The K axiom encoded in our elimination rule: `(box-elim N M)` — from $\Box(P \to Q)$ at step N and $\Box P$ at step M, derive $\Box Q$. The necessitation rule is our introduction rule: `(box-intro N)` — from $\emptyset \vdash P$ at step N, derive $\Box P$.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: And the diamond?

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: $\Diamond$ is defined, not primitive. `(diamond-def N)` unfolds $\Diamond P$ to $\lnot \Box \lnot P$. `(diamond-def-rev N)` folds it back. Two directions, one definition.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: So the diamond is [syntactic-sugar|syntactic sugar] — a convenient way to speak of possibility, but at its core, nothing more than the failure of necessary falsehood.

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: Exactly. And one more thing: in System K, modal logic is [propositional|propositional]. You cannot quantify into modal contexts — no $\forall x.\; \Box P(x)$. The box and diamond sit atop propositional logic, not first-order.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: So the quantifier rules are gated — available only in first-order mode, not here.

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: Precisely. But what we gain is the ability to reason about what must be, what could be, and what cannot be — across every possible configuration of reality.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: We are no longer speaking only about what is true.

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: We are speaking about what truth survives, across every door between worlds.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: Then what happened to propositional logic?

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: It never left. It was simply… beneath the surface.

lines.speaker: Curry
lines.text: The engine still runs on PL. Every rule you have used — implication, contradiction, even substitution — is still compiled into propositional inference steps.

lines.speaker: C. I. Lewis
lines.text: Then modal logic did not replace it?

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: No. It extends it. But extensions are only notation. The machine still executes propositional transitions.

lines.speaker: Curry
lines.text: Even $(\Box(P \to Q)) \to (\Box P \to \Box Q)$ is not primitive. It is encoded as a transformation over PL derivations indexed by worlds.

lines.speaker: De Morgan
lines.text: So the rules we thought were new… were already there, hidden inside the system?

lines.speaker: Curry
lines.text: Exactly. System K was never added. It was uncovered.

lines.speaker: Saul Kripke
lines.text: The worlds are aligned. The rules are silent. What remains is not explanation, but action. Step forward — and let reasoning begin.
